behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is primarily handler decision logic (verify HMAC result -> reject -> do not forward), which can fail inside one object boundary. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess is required to reproduce this failure. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection-gate: I considered Integration because forwarding touches another module, but the behavior text is disqualified for higher level by the explicit single-decision logic: "signature does not match" -> "401" and "not forwarded"; a unit test with a spy/stub forwarder can catch real regressions.
behaviors.test_description: Call the webhook handler with a fixed raw body and a mismatched X-Signature-256 header, using an in-memory response double and a spy queue/forwarder. Assert status 401 and zero enqueue/forward calls.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is an ordering/handoff bug across modules (handler response path vs persistent queue write). Unit stubs can miss persistence-path sequencing bugs. REAL-INFRA: this does not require real external network/OS primitives beyond local module wiring and local persistence. Integration-brake: (a) no required real OS/network/subprocess boundary in behavior text, (b) no AI output dependence, (c) no multi-agentic chain, (d) no LLM stage handoff. Rejection-gate: I considered System due the word "persistent," but behavior text only requires write-before-ack ordering, not real external infra semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Run the handler wired to the real queue module against a local persistence backend (e.g., sqlite/file-backed test store). Delay queue commit completion; assert HTTP 200 is not sent until enqueue resolves, and persisted record exists when 200 is observed.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is cross-module orchestration (ingest/queue boundary vs downstream worker completion), not just a pure function. REAL-INFRA: no real external OS/network requirement to expose the bug; in-process modules with controlled async behavior are sufficient. Integration-brake: (a) no mandatory real subprocess/network in wording, (b) no model output dependency, (c) no multi-agentic sequence, (d) no LLM orchestration. Rejection-gate: I considered System for real HTTP timing, but behavior text is about wait semantics (not end-to-end infra conditions), so Integration is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + downstream processor module in-process. Make downstream processing block (or resolve late) after enqueue. Assert HTTP response is sent before downstream completion signal.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: this explicitly requires wall-clock latency under concurrency and slow prior work; real server runtime, scheduler/event-loop behavior, and real HTTP timing can produce failures hidden by in-process mocks/fake timers. MOCK-HIDE: mocked transport/clock often masks queue contention and runtime starvation. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real Node service process and send concurrent HTTP webhook requests while earlier events are intentionally slow in downstream processing. Measure actual wall-clock response times; assert each new webhook response is <=3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: dedup correctness is state handoff across requests (handler -> persistence/queue uniqueness state), which is a module boundary failure. Unit-only tests can miss real state-store interaction bugs. REAL-INFRA: can be validated with local persistence wiring; no required external OS/network/subprocess. Integration-brake: (a) no mandatory real external infra, (b) no AI output, (c) no agentic chain, (d) no LLM stages. Rejection-gate: I considered System for multi-request behavior, but behavior text only requires duplicate-ID suppression at queue write boundary, which local integration can verify.
behaviors.test_description: With real handler + real persistent queue module in a test store, submit same event ID twice. Assert first enqueue succeeds and second is skipped/rejected such that only one persisted queue record exists.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is an error-contract handoff between queue module and HTTP layer (capacity signal -> status/header mapping). A pure unit can miss mismatched error types/contracts across modules. REAL-INFRA: no external network/OS/subprocess is required; in-process module wiring is enough. Integration-brake: (a) no required real external infra in behavior text, (b) no AI output dependence, (c) no multi-agentic chain, (d) no LLM orchestration. Rejection-gate: I considered Unit, but behavior explicitly states cross-module condition "queue reports it is full," so Integration is the safer minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real handler with queue module configured to return a concrete "full" condition. Send webhook request and assert response is 503 and includes a valid Retry-After header.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this behavior targets queue implementation + persistence interaction under configured limits; mocking queue would trivially pass and hide real enforcement failures. REAL-INFRA: can be exercised with local persistence backend and queue module wiring; external OS/network/subprocess is not inherently required by wording. Integration-brake: (a) no explicit need for real external infra, (b) no AI dependency, (c) no agentic chain, (d) no LLM orchestration. Rejection-gate: I considered System because persistence can involve infra details, but behavior text only requires configured-capacity enforcement in the queue component, which local integration can verify.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue against its test persistence backend with capacity N, enqueue N events, then enqueue one more. Assert the final enqueue is rejected and stored count remains N.
